fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up the speech coordinator work we've been scoping out. The gist is: we want a clean interface that handles the whole lifecycle of a voice recognition session — starting it up, feeding it audio events from the underlying platform, and shutting it down gracefully. Think of it like that channel-wrapper pattern we used in the notification module a while back, but for speech.

The tricky parts are around state management: the coordinator should know whether it's been set up yet, whether it's actively listening, and it should track the last thing it heard plus some basic audio level info. We also need it to handle errors smartly — some errors are 'just retry' situations and others are 'something is broken, stop everything' situations, and the behavior when a session is already cancelled is a bit nuanced (the team had opinions about this in the last standup).

There's also a locale/language selection piece — the platform hands us a raw list and we need to parse it into something usable, sorted in a sensible way for display, but the 'default' choice follows a different rule than the display order.

Please make sure the value objects (transcription candidates, results, errors) all support proper equality checks and can round-trip through the wire format. The confidence scoring logic has a special case for when the platform doesn't give us a score at all — make sure that edge case is handled consistently everywhere.

One other thing to pin down from the questions that came in: for confidence handling, the reserved “missing score” value is -1. If a candidate comes through with confidence -1, that means the platform did not report a confidence score, so it should always return has_rating=false and it should still be treated as confident no matter what acceptance threshold is in play. The normal default threshold is 0.8, so a candidate is confident when its score is >= 0.8, unless it has the missing sentinel -1, and callers can pass a different threshold if they want to override that default.

On result behavior, equality is intentionally narrow: two recognition results are equal when their best transcription, meaning the first alternate's phrase, and their final flag match. Anything after the first alternate does not affect equality or hashing, and equal results need to produce equal hash codes too. Also, if a recognition result has no alternates at all, then the best transcription is the empty string, the confidence is zero (0.0), it is not considered confident, and has_rating=false.

For the event stream, there are four recognition event kinds and each one only carries the payload for its own kind: 'status' with a listening boolean, 'final_result' or 'result' with a RecognitionResult, 'error' with a RecognitionError, and 'sound_level' with a float loudness level. And for the coordinator snapshot, the baseline before any initialization or listening should be exactly listening=false, recognized=false, available=false, has_error=false, last_recognized='' (empty string), last_status='' (empty string), last_level=0.0.

A couple lifecycle specifics too: both stop and cancel should only call into the platform if the coordinator has already been initialized. Cancel also immediately marks the session as not listening anymore, so listening=false in the state snapshot. Stop does call the platform stop, but it does not have to force the same listening update right away since that part may get resolved by later platform notifications. The listen/start-listening path can take an optional locale identifier; if one is provided, pass it through to the platform listen call, and if not, call the platform without one, reported as locale=none. If the platform listen call fails, normalize that into 'listen_failed' and keep the platform-provided failure detail string attached.

Just to restate the wrapper we’re talking about so nobody drifts: this is the SpeechRecognitionCoordinator class that wraps the async platform message channel, managing session state and routing platform notifications (recognition, status, sound, error) to registered callbacks. And on the cancellation nuance from standup, the rule is the one from feature6_8_error_handling: explicitly user-cancelled sessions suppress subsequent platform errors, but implicitly cancelled sessions (via cancel-on-error) do NOT suppress further errors.

trigger_keywords: locale sort order
how are locales ordered in the returned list
display order of supported locales
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The parsed locale list returned to callers is sorted alphabetically by display name. However, the system (default) locale is always the first well-formed entry from the ORIGINAL unsorted platform order — not the first entry after sorting. These two may differ when the platform list is not already sorted.

trigger_keywords: malformed locale entries
invalid locale string handling
locale parsing rejects bad entries
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Locale entries are colon-separated 'identifier:display name' strings. Any entry that does not have exactly one identifier and one display name (i.e., not exactly one colon producing two non-empty parts) is silently dropped. Only well-formed entries are included in the parsed list.

trigger_keywords: initialization idempotency
calling initialize twice
does init invoke platform again if already initialized
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Initialization is idempotent. Once the coordinator has been successfully initialized, calling initialize again returns success immediately without re-invoking the platform initialize operation (invoked=false on the second call). If the first initialization failed, the platform would be retried on a subsequent call.

trigger_keywords: listen before initialization
start listening without init
what error when not yet initialized
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Attempting to start listening (or query locales) before the coordinator has been successfully initialized is rejected with a normalized 'not_initialized' error. The platform listen operation is never invoked in this case.

trigger_keywords: cancel on error behavior
permanent error stops session
when does error end the session automatically
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A permanent error only causes an automatic session cancellation when the session was started with cancel-on-error enabled. Without cancel-on-error, even a permanent error is forwarded to the error callback but the session keeps listening. A transient error never stops the session regardless of the cancel-on-error setting.

trigger_keywords: error after explicit cancel
suppress error when cancelled
deliver error to already-cancelled session
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Once a session has been explicitly cancelled by the user, any subsequently delivered error from the platform is suppressed — it is not forwarded to the error callback. However, when a cancel was triggered implicitly by cancel-on-error, further delivered errors are still forwarded normally.

trigger_keywords: wire format for recognition candidate
JSON field names for candidate serialization
how is a candidate represented on the wire
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A recognition candidate's wire (JSON) shape uses 'recognizedWords' for the transcribed phrase and 'confidence' for the score. Example: {"recognizedWords":"hello","confidence":0.85}. Deserialization reads those fields; serialization produces the identical shape.

trigger_keywords: recognition result wire format
JSON fields for result serialization
finalResult field name in wire payload
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A recognition result's wire (JSON) shape uses 'alternates' (an array of candidate wire objects) and 'finalResult' (boolean). Example: {"alternates":[{"recognizedWords":"hello","confidence":0.85}],"finalResult":false}. The field is named 'finalResult' on the wire, not 'final'.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'candidate_confidence', 'candidate_json', 'candidate_equality', 'result_summary', 'result_json', 'result_equality', 'error_json', 'error_equality', 'event', 'locales', 'locales_uninitialized', 'session'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the 'default' choice follows a different rule than the display order
context_pointers.actual_meaning: feature5_1_locale_parsing: system locale = first well-formed entry in original platform order; display list = sorted by display name — these are two independent orderings

context_pointers.vague_reference: confidence scoring logic has a special case for when the platform doesn't give us a score
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The missing sentinel value -1 in RecognitionCandidate: confidence == -1 means has_rating=false and is_confident=true unconditionally, bypassing the threshold comparison

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect — please check the existing codebase (especially the coordinator's session state tracking and the platform channel handler) and the test cases under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the authoritative behavior. If something still isn't clear, flag it and the team can clarify in the next sync.

feature1_1_candidate_confidence.json.description: A recognition candidate pairs a transcribed phrase with a confidence score. Evaluate whether the candidate should be treated as confident. A candidate is confident when its confidence score meets or exceeds an acceptance threshold (defaulting to a standard value when none is supplied), or when its confidence is the reserved 'missing' sentinel (meaning the platform did not provide a score, in which case the candidate is always treated as confident). The output reports the transcribed phrase, the numeric confidence, whether a real confidence rating is present (false only for the missing sentinel), and the confident verdict.

feature1_2_candidate_serialization.json.description: A recognition candidate is reconstructed from its wire payload, a JSON object carrying the transcribed phrase and confidence score, and can be serialized back to the same wire shape. The output reports the parsed phrase and confidence and the re-serialized JSON object, demonstrating a lossless round trip.

feature1_3_candidate_equality.json.description: Recognition candidates compare equal when both their transcribed phrase and their confidence score match, and equal candidates hash alike. The output reports whether two candidates are equal and whether their hashes agree.

feature2_1_result_summary.json.description: A recognition result holds an ordered list of alternative candidate transcriptions plus a flag marking whether it is the final result for the utterance. The best transcription and its confidence are derived from the first alternate; when there are no alternates the best transcription is the empty string, the confidence is zero, the result is not considered confident, and it has no confidence rating. The output reports the best transcription, derived confidence, final flag, confident verdict, rating presence, the number of alternates, and each alternate's phrase and confidence in order.

cases.expected_output:
recognized=hello
confidence=0.85
final=true
confident=true
has_rating=true
alternate_count=2
alternate[0]=hello@0.85
alternate[1]=hello there@0.62

feature2_2_result_serialization.json.description: A recognition result is reconstructed from its wire payload, a JSON object carrying the ordered alternates array and the final flag, and can be serialized back to the same wire shape. The output reports the derived best transcription, its confidence, the final flag, and the re-serialized JSON object.

feature2_3_result_equality.json.description: Recognition results compare equal when their best transcription (the first alternate's phrase) and their final flag match; the remaining alternates do not affect equality, and equal results hash alike. The output reports whether two results are equal and whether their hashes agree.

feature3_1_error_serialization.json.description: A recognition error carries a short error identifier and a permanence flag that states whether recognition can continue (transient) or is blocked until the condition is resolved (permanent). It is reconstructed from its wire payload, a JSON object with the identifier and permanence flag, and serialized back to the same shape. The output reports the identifier, the permanence flag, and the re-serialized JSON object.

feature3_2_error_equality.json.description: Recognition errors compare equal when both their error identifier and permanence flag match, and equal errors hash alike. The output reports whether two errors are equal and whether their hashes agree.

feature4_recognition_event.json.description: A recognition event is a tagged envelope delivered on the event stream. Its kind selects which payload is meaningful: a status event exposes the listening flag, a final-result event exposes the recognition result, an error event exposes the recognition error, and a sound-level event exposes the input loudness level. The output reports the event kind and the accessor relevant to that kind.

feature5_1_locale_parsing.json.description: The set of supported recognition locales is supplied by the platform as a list of colon-separated 'identifier:display name' strings. Each well-formed entry becomes a locale with an identifier and a display name; entries that are not exactly one identifier and one name are dropped. The returned list is ordered by display name. The system (default) locale is the first well-formed entry in the ORIGINAL platform order (before the display-name sort). The output reports the count of parsed locales, each parsed locale's identifier and display name in returned order, and the system locale's identifier.

cases.expected_output:
count=2
locale[0]=en_US|English US
locale[1]=fr_CA|French Canada
system=en_US

feature5_2_locale_requires_init.json.description: Querying the supported locales before the coordinator has been initialized is rejected: the operation reports a normalized not-initialized error rather than reaching the platform.

feature6_1_permission.json.description: The coordinator can report whether microphone permission has already been granted by querying the platform, without prompting the user. The output reports the permission verdict returned by the platform.

feature6_2_initialization.json.description: Initialization prepares the coordinator and returns whether it succeeded. It is idempotent: once it has succeeded, calling it again returns success WITHOUT re-invoking the platform initialize operation. When the platform reports failure, initialization returns failure and the coordinator remains unavailable. The output reports, per initialize call, the returned result and whether the platform initialize was invoked, followed by an availability snapshot.

cases.expected_output:
init result=true invoked=true
init result=true invoked=false
listening=false recognized=false available=true has_error=false last_recognized= last_status= last_level=0.0

feature6_3_listen.json.description: Starting a listening session requires a prior successful initialization; otherwise it is rejected with a normalized not-initialized error and the platform listen is never invoked. After successful initialization, listening invokes the platform listen operation; an optional locale identifier is forwarded to the platform when provided, and is absent otherwise. If the platform raises a listen failure, it is translated into a normalized listen-failed error that carries the platform-supplied failure detail. The output reports, per step, the initialize result and the listen outcome (invoked flag and forwarded locale, or the normalized error).

cases.expected_output:
init result=true invoked=true
listen invoked=true locale=none

init result=true invoked=true
listen error=listen_failed details=Device Listen Failure

feature6_4_recognition_callback.json.description: While listening, each recognition payload delivered by the platform is parsed into a recognition result and handed to the registered result callback, and the coordinator remembers the most recently recognized phrase. Multiple deliveries invoke the callback once each in order, and the remembered phrase tracks the latest delivery. The output reports each callback invocation (recognized phrase and final flag) followed by a state snapshot whose remembered phrase reflects the last delivery.

cases.expected_output:
init result=true invoked=true
listen invoked=true locale=none
result recognized=hello final=false
listening=true recognized=true available=true has_error=false last_recognized=hello last_status=listening last_level=0.0

feature6_5_status_callback.json.description: A status update delivered by the platform is forwarded to the registered status callback with the new status string. The output reports the forwarded status.

feature6_6_sound_level_callback.json.description: While a listening session is active, a sound-level update delivered by the platform is forwarded to the registered sound-level callback and recorded as the last sound level. The output reports the forwarded level followed by a state snapshot whose last sound level reflects the delivery.

cases.expected_output:
init result=true invoked=true
listen invoked=true locale=none
sound_level 0.5
listening=true recognized=false available=true has_error=false last_recognized= last_status=listening last_level=0.5

feature6_7_stop_cancel.json.description: Stopping or cancelling does nothing when the coordinator was never initialized: the corresponding platform operation is not invoked. After a successful initialization and an active listening session, stopping invokes the platform stop operation, and cancelling invokes the platform cancel operation and leaves the session no longer listening. The output reports whether the platform operation was invoked, and a state snapshot where relevant.

cases.expected_output:
init result=true invoked=true
listen invoked=true locale=none
cancel invoked=true
listening=false recognized=false available=true has_error=false last_recognized= last_status=listening last_level=0.0

init result=true invoked=true
listen invoked=true locale=none
stop invoked=true

feature6_8_error_handling.json.description: An error delivered by the platform during an active session is forwarded to the registered error callback. A transient error does not stop the session. A permanent error stops the session ONLY when the session was started with cancel-on-error enabled; otherwise the session keeps listening. Once the session has been explicitly cancelled by the user, a subsequently delivered error is suppressed (not forwarded). When cancel-on-error triggers an implicit cancel, a further delivered error is still forwarded. The output reports each forwarded error (identifier and permanence) interleaved with state snapshots as specified by the steps.

cases.expected_output:
init result=true invoked=true
listen invoked=true locale=none
error msg=network permanent=true
listening=true recognized=false available=true has_error=true last_recognized= last_status=listening last_level=0.0

init result=true invoked=true
listen invoked=true locale=none
cancel invoked=true
listening=false recognized=false available=true has_error=false last_recognized= last_status=listening last_level=0.0

feature6_9_initial_state.json.description: Before any initialization or listening, the coordinator exposes a well-defined starting snapshot: it is not listening, has recognized nothing, is unavailable, has no error, has an empty remembered phrase, an empty last status, and a zero last sound level.

cases.expected_output: listening=false recognized=false available=false has_error=false last_recognized= last_status= last_level=0.0
